items.correct: Attributing human emotions or qualities to nature or inanimate objects to reflect a character's feelings or scene's mood (e.g. angry storms during conflict).
items.misconception: Pathetic fallacy is when an argument in a story is weak or sad.
items.random_wrong: Pathetic fallacy is when characters cry over silly things.
items.why: The term uses 'pathetic' in its old sense — pertaining to emotion (pathos) — not 'feeble'. Students misread it as a value judgement when it is a technical device coined by Ruskin.

items.q: Why does historical context matter when reading literature?
items.correct: Texts emerge from and respond to specific historical conditions — context illuminates references, assumptions and arguments that might otherwise be invisible.
items.misconception: Context just means writing about what the author had for breakfast.
items.random_wrong: Context is required because all texts are about history.
items.why: Students treat context as biographical trivia. Useful context is what the text engages with — politics, ideology, genre conventions — not personal detail of the author's life.

items.correct: Subtle hints or clues earlier in a text that anticipate later events, making the resolution feel both surprising and inevitable.
items.misconception: Foreshadowing is when a character predicts the future.
items.random_wrong: Foreshadowing is the shadow a character casts in a scene.
items.why: Foreshadowing works on the reader, not within the story world. A character's prediction is a plot event — foreshadowing is an authorial technique embedded in detail and tone.

items.q: Who coined the term unreliable narrator in 1961?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Wayne Booth. “Northrop Frye” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which Marquez novel exemplifies magic realism?
items.misconception: Love in the Time of Cholera
items.why: The answer examiners want is: One Hundred Years of Solitude. “Love in the Time of Cholera” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Marquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude published?
items.why: The real figure is 1967 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
